Name: infotheoreticrateoptimization
Title: Information-Theoretic Rate Optimization for Adaptive Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: Can we develop an information-theoretic framework to dynamically optimize the bit-rate allocation for model compression, ensuring minimal distortion in model performance while maximizing compression efficiency?
Related Work: Recent works such as Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019) have explored the use of rate-distortion theory to understand and optimize model compression. However, these works often focus on fixed-rate compression schemes or specific pruning methods. Our proposal differs by introducing a dynamic rate optimization framework that adaptively allocates bits based on the task-relevant information content of different model components, ensuring minimal distortion while achieving high compression.
Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for adaptive model compression that dynamically optimizes the bit-rate allocation to minimize distortion in model performance. Unlike existing methods that often use fixed-rate schemes or heuristic-based pruning, our approach leverages rate-distortion theory to allocate bits where they reduce task-relevant distortion the most. We derive an achievable rate-distortion bound and propose an algorithm to iteratively refine the bit allocation. We validate our method on vision transformers and large language models, demonstrating significant improvements in compression efficiency while maintaining or even improving model performance.
Experiments: Derive an achievable rate-distortion bound for model compression using mutual information and Fisher information to quantify task-relevant distortion.
Develop an algorithm that iteratively refines the bit allocation for different model components, ensuring minimal distortion in model performance.
Evaluate the proposed method on vision transformers (e.g., ViT, DeiT) and large language models (e.g., LLaMA) using standard benchmarks such as ImageNet and WikiText-103.
Compare the compression efficiency and model performance of our method against state-of-the-art fixed-rate and adaptive compression techniques.
Analyze the impact of different distortion metrics (e.g., cross-entropy loss, mean squared error) on the compression efficiency and model performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational complexity of estimating mutual information and Fisher information could be high, especially for large models.
The derived rate-distortion bounds may not be tight in practice, leading to suboptimal bit allocation.
The dynamic bit allocation algorithm may require careful tuning to balance between compression efficiency and model performance.
The method's effectiveness may vary across different model architectures and tasks, requiring further investigation.
